text.tspan: VALET KEY: client wants to upload/download a file directly to/from storage.
Instead of proxying through your app (slow + expensive), hand them a
short-lived, narrowly-scoped credential and let them talk to storage directly.

g.text: NAIVE APPROACH (don't do this for large files)

text.tspan: PROPERTIES OF A VALET KEY
u2022 SHORT-LIVED (minutes, not days)
u2022 SCOPED to one resource (this exact object key)
u2022 SCOPED to one operation (PUT only, or GET only)
u2022 ENFORCED CONSTRAINTS (max size, content-type, IP, etc.)
u2022 NOT REVOCABLE before expiry u2192 keep expiry short
u2022 NOT cacheable / shareable (or treat any sharing as a breach)
IMPLEMENTATIONS
u2022 AWS S3 presigned URLs (the canonical implementation)
u2022 Azure Blob Storage SAS (Shared Access Signatures)
u2022 GCP Cloud Storage signed URLs
u2022 Cloudflare R2 / Backblaze B2 presigned URLs
u2022 AWS STS AssumeRole (short-lived AWS credentials)
u2022 OAuth scope-limited tokens
Client u2192 App u2192 Storage
1. Client uploads 5 GB to your app
2. App holds it in memory / temp
3. App uploads it to S3
u2192 doubles bandwidth (egress costs add up)
u2192 app server memory/time tied up
u2192 doesn't scale; slow

g.text: 1. Client asks app: 'I want to upload my-file.jpg (5 MB)'
2. App AUTHORIZES the request (is user allowed? quota? content type?)

text.tspan: 3. App generates a PRESIGNED URL via storage SDK:
uploads/abc-123.jpg
method=PUT
expires=5 minutes
max-size=10MB
content-type=image/jpeg
uploader-id=user-42 (server enforced)
[uses app's storage credentials to sign;
the URL itself is the only thing the client gets]

g.text: 4. App returns the URL to the client

text.tspan: 5. Client uploads file DIRECTLY to storage at that URL
(or downloads from a presigned GET URL)
6. Storage validates signature + constraints + expiry,
accepts (or rejects)
